phase1.explanation:
Iterators provide a way to traverse collections:

Iterator:
• hasNext() — check if more elements
• next() — get next element
• remove() — removes current element (optional)

ListIterator (extends Iterator):
• hasPrevious() — check if previous elements exist
• previous() — get previous element
• set(element) — replaces current element
• add(element) — inserts before current

For-each loop:
• for (Type variable : collection)
• Syntactic sugar for iterator
• Cannot remove elements

Key differences:
• For-each: read-only, simple syntax
• Iterator: can remove, more control
• ListIterator: bidirectional, can add/set

ConcurrentModificationException:
• Occurs when modifying collection during iteration
• Iterator's remove() is the safe way to remove
• removeIf() is another safe option

When to use:
• For-each: simple iteration
• Iterator: need to remove elements
• ListIterator: need to traverse backwards or modify

⚠️ for-each with arrays is different from collections — uses simple index, no iterator created

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates a LinkedList<Integer> with numbers 1-10
2. Uses an Iterator to remove all even numbers
3. Uses a ListIterator to add 0 at the beginning
4. Uses a ListIterator to add 11 at the end
5. Prints the final list

Bonus: Use for-each to print each element after modifications.
dialogueHints: Iterator: while (it.hasNext()) { int n = it.next(); if (n % 2 == 0) it.remove(); }
To add at end: use listIt.add(11) after the loop or list.add(11)
